chunk_summary: Defines acute malnutrition in Indian children using anthropometric criteria, outlines care levels for MAM and SAM, and emphasizes structured follow-up and community linkage through ICDS and Anganwadi centers.
learning_objectives: Understand the anthropometric criteria for diagnosing MAM and SAM in children under 5 years.
Differentiate between outpatient and facility-based care for acute malnutrition based on complications.
Learn the follow-up and escalation triggers for malnourished children in community and facility settings.
key_facts: MAM is defined as weight-for-length/height z-score between -2 and -3 SD.
SAM is defined as weight-for-length/height z-score <-3 SD, MUAC <115 mm (6–59 months), or bilateral pitting edema.
Uncomplicated SAM can be managed in the community if a structured program exists; otherwise, refer to a facility.
Infants <6 months who are visibly wasted or too weak to suckle require urgent facility assessment.
Follow-up weekly initially; escalate care if temperature ≥39°C, hypothermia, new edema, or weight loss for two consecutive weeks.
reference_quote: Use under-5 anthropometry to define severity: MAM is weight-for-length/height z-score between -2 and -3 SD, and SAM is <-3 SD, or MUAC <115 mm (6–59 months), or bilateral pitting edema.
document_key_topics: Anthropometric criteria for MAM and SAM
Outpatient vs. facility-based care for malnutrition
Community linkage through ICDS and Anganwadi centers
